Retail Access Vault Verdict Why Alt-1 How it works Money flow Functions 8 Scenarios See it run Under the hood Fee model Scope & next
Proof of Feasibility · Local POC · Alternative 1

The Retail Access mechanism works.

An ERC-7540-style vault that pools retail USDC, wraps an illiquid private-credit fund, and serves permissionless subscriptions & redemptions through a queue + peer-to-peer matching + 3-layer redemption protocol. This POC proves the mechanism is implementable and behaves exactly as specified.

FEASIBLE · 8 / 8 mechanism scenarios verified
8 / 8acceptance scenarios pass
36automated tests green
2production contracts
1local Anvil chain
The hard parts work. P2P matching nets subscriptions against redemptions; the 3-layer fallback sources cash in the right order.
No money is lost. Across every flow, including failure and wind-down, funds are fully accounted for and no user is stranded.
It's buildable. Two clean contracts, exact-number tests, ~1 senior engineer, matching the original 7-day estimate.

The problem & why Alternative 1 For decision-makers

Private credit funds (e.g. Hamilton Lane Evergreen) have high ticket minimums and only open subscription/redemption windows periodically, out of reach for retail. The vault sits in front: it pools retail USDC, hits the minimum ticket, holds the wrapped position in custody, and keeps a ~20% liquid buffer to absorb everyday redemptions. Only when the buffer is exhausted does it touch the illiquid fund.

The PRD offered two designs for how assets are held. We built Alt-1 (custody we own) because every component is something we can reason about end-to-end, the cleanest subject for proving the mechanism.

How it works Everyone

Two production contracts with a clean split: the Vault owns the logic and state, the Custody owns the tokens. Everything runs on a single local chain; the external fund, stablecoin, liquid sleeve and swap venue are mocked.

1 · Async queue (ERC-7540)

Deposits and redemptions are request → queue → fulfil-at-epoch, so the protocol can aggregate small tickets and only hit the fund's periodic window once per epoch.

2 · Peer-to-peer matching

At each epoch, subscriptions and redemptions net off directly. Matched volume costs nothing, with no fund round-trip and no swap fees.

3 · 3-layer redemption

Cash is sourced in strict order: matching → liquid buffer → illiquid fund. The expensive illiquid layer only fires when the buffer is short.

4 · Rebalance & NAV

Net subscriptions buy the under-allocated sleeve to drift back toward 80/20. Redemption value uses the NAV at epoch time, submitted by the admin before each epoch.

Who controls what. A single owner (no role-based access in this POC) configures the launchpad, calls processEpoch and triggerWindDown, and submits the NAV through setPrice before each epoch. That manual NAV is the core trust assumption: the Vault reads it through a narrow INavSource seam, so a real Pruv feed or an oracle can replace it later without touching Vault logic.

What each state allows, and who can act

Transitions are one-way. Each state opens a narrow set of calls; everything else reverts. Actor tags: ADMIN the single owner, USER any retail wallet, ANYONE permissionless.

Initialized exit: launchpad start time

ADMIN initLaunchpad, configAsset · set timing, minimum, 80/20 target

LaunchpadStart exit: launchpad end time

USER depositToLaunchpad · lock USDC into the raise
ANYONE transitionAfterDeadline · settle the raise once time is up

LaunchpadFail terminal, refunds only

USER refundLaunchpad · minimum missed, reclaim USDC

EpochBased exit: admin triggerWindDown

USER requestDeposit, requestRedeem, cancelRequest, claim
ADMIN setPrice (NAV), processEpoch · the repeating tick

WindDown exit: all redemptions settled

ADMIN setPrice, then settle: refund subs, liquidate buffer, redeem fund
USER claim · collect final pro-rata USDC

Closed terminal

NONE lifecycle complete; a new fund needs a fresh deployment

Follow the money Everyone

What actually happens to a user's USDC, end to end. A deposit becomes shares through the queue and an epoch; a redemption turns shares back into USDC, sourced in a strict order. Three retail users (Alice, Bob, Charlie) only ever call the Vault; a single admin operates the epochs.

Before any epoch runs, the vault must be born: a launchpad pools retail USDC until it clears the fund's minimum ticket. All-or-nothing: if it misses the minimum, every depositor is refunded.

GENESIS
Retail USDC
→
depositToLaunchpadpooled until the deadline
→
minimum ticket met?all-or-nothing test
→
buy 80/20, mint sharesor refund if it missed
DEPOSIT
User USDC
→
requestDepositlocked in queue, cancelable
→
processEpochmatch P2P, then buy toward 80/20
→
rACCESS sharesminted, then claim
REDEEM
rACCESS shares
→
requestRedeemlocked in queue, cancelable
→
processEpochmatch → buffer → fund
→
USDC back to walletat epoch-time NAV

Requests are queued, not instant: value is fixed at the epoch (the rate is frozen), then the user claims. Anything still pending can be cancelled (ERC-7887).

The tokens involved

TokenWhat it representsHeld byDecimals
USDCthe stablecoin users bring in and receive backusers, then Vault / Custody6
rACCESS sharesthe vault token: a claim on a pro-rata slice of all assetsusers18
wRWAthe wrapped fund position, the ~80% illiquid sleeveCustody6
Liquid tokenthe ~20% buffer, swappable to USDC anytimeCustody6

All four are mocks in this POC. wRWA and the liquid token are 6-decimal so the cross-decimal NAV math stays exact (see Under the hood); only the vault share is 18-decimal.

The two mechanics, visualized

Peer-to-peer matching. Subscriptions and redemptions in the same epoch cancel out first, so only the surplus reaches the fund.

Subscribe 10,000
match 4,000net 6,000 invested
Redeem 4,000
match 4,000

The matched 4,000 nets directly between users: no fund round-trip, no swap. (Scenario S4.)

3-layer redemption. Cash for a withdrawal is drawn in a strict order, cheapest first. The illiquid fund is the last resort.

1MatchingP2P, free, instant
2Liquid bufferswap the ~20% sleeve
3Illiquid fundredeem from Pruv, last resort

Each layer fires only if the one above is exhausted. (Scenarios S5, S6.)

Custody: where the assets actually move

Vault holds state; Custody holds tokens. Every external token move is one of four Custody calls (onlyVault). Idle USDC is converted into the two sleeves on the way in, and back into USDC on the way out.

 IN  (net subscription)                          OUT (net redemption)
 ----------------------                          --------------------
 idle USDC - subscribeToPruv  -> wRWA   (~80%)    wRWA   - redeemFromPruv   -> USDC
 idle USDC - swapUSDCForLiquid -> liquid (~20%)    liquid - swapLiquidForUSDC -> USDC

 totalAssets() = wRWA (x Pruv price) + liquid     both valued in 6-dec USDC

On a net subscription the vault buys the under-allocated sleeve first (rebalance toward 80/20). On a net redemption it sells in the 3-layer order above: liquid before fund.

The main functions Tech

The public surface, grouped by phase. User calls are permissionless; admin calls are the single owner; Custody calls are internal (onlyVault). Tags: ADMIN USER ANYONE INTERNAL.

FunctionWhoWhat it does
Setup · Initialized
initLaunchpadADMINSet the launchpad window (start/end) and the minimum raise.
configAssetADMINWire Pruv / liquid buffer / AMM and set the 80/20 target.
Launchpad
depositToLaunchpadUSERLock USDC into the raise.
transitionAfterDeadlineANYONESettle the raise: if the minimum is met, buy 80/20 and go EpochBased; otherwise LaunchpadFail.
claimLaunchpadSharesUSERMint your launch shares pro-rata (first epoch only).
refundLaunchpadUSERReclaim your USDC if the raise failed.
Epoch · users
requestDepositUSERQueue USDC to be converted into shares at the next epoch.
requestRedeemUSERQueue shares to be redeemed for USDC at the next epoch.
cancelRequestUSERCancel a still-pending request before the epoch processes (ERC-7887).
claimUSERAfter the epoch, pull your minted shares or settled USDC to your wallet.
Settlement · admin
setPrice (Pruv)ADMINSubmit the current NAV before each tick (manual price, see Under the hood).
processEpochADMINThe tick: match P2P, rebalance / source assets, settle both queues, then epoch + 1.
triggerWindDownADMINStart the one-way orderly shutdown.
Custody · internal (onlyVault)
subscribeToPruvINTERNALConvert idle USDC into wRWA (buy the illiquid sleeve).
redeemFromPruvINTERNALConvert wRWA back into USDC (sell illiquid, the last-resort layer).
swapUSDCForLiquid / swapLiquidForUSDCINTERNALSwap between idle USDC and the liquid buffer (1:1 via the mock AMM).
depositUSDC / withdrawUSDCINTERNALMove USDC into or out of custody.
Views
state · nav · totalAssets · currentEpochREADLifecycle state, price per share, assets in custody (6-dec USDC), epoch counter.
pendingSubAmount · pendingRedeemSharesREADA user's not-yet-settled deposit USDC / redeem shares.

What we proved: the 8 scenarios Everyone

Each scenario is the acceptance criterion for one part of the mechanism. Every one is an automated test asserting exact numbers, and a branch of the live demo. Below: what each proves and the concrete figures (at NAV 1.00 unless noted).

PASS
S1

Happy path: full lifecycle

Money flows launchpad → epoch → wind-down with nothing lost.
Launchpad 100,000 → 80,000 illiquid + 20,000 liquid Epoch: matched 5,000 P2P; net sub 5,000 invested Wind-down: all 3 holders made whole; supply → 0
PASS
S2

Launchpad fail + refund

Below the minimum, every depositor is refunded 100%.
Locked 30,000 < 50,000 minimum → LaunchpadFail Alice refunded 30,000 → balance fully restored Custody holds 0
PASS
S3

Cancel pending subscription

A user can withdraw a queued request before it's processed (ERC-7887).
Alice requests 10,000, then cancels → USDC returned processEpoch runs as a clean no-op queue integrity preserved
PASS
S4

Matching: subscription > redemption

Subscriptions/redemptions net off P2P; only the surplus hits the fund.
Sub 10,000 vs redeem 4,000 → matched 4,000 P2P Net sub 6,000 → buy 4,800 illiquid + 1,200 liquid Saved a 4,000 fund round-trip
PASS
S5

Matching: redemption > subscription

Excess redemptions are paid from the liquid buffer, not the fund.
Sub 4,000 vs redeem 10,000 → matched 4,000 Net redeem 6,000 from buffer (20,000 → 14,000) Illiquid fund untouched
PASS
S6

Redemption needs illiquid fallback

When the buffer is exhausted, redemption falls through to the fund.
Buffer drained to 2,000; redeem 8,000 Layer 2: 2,000 liquid · Layer 3: 6,000 from fund Alice receives 8,000 across both layers
PASS
S7

NAV change drives payout

When the underlying appreciates, redeemers are paid the higher NAV.
setPrice +10% → assets 108,000, NAV 1.08 Redeem 10 shares → 10.80 USDC (not 10.00) value taken at epoch-time NAV
PASS
S8

Wind-down mid-epoch

Wind-down cleanly settles all in-flight obligations; no user stranded.
In-flight sub 5,000 + redeem 5,000 shares Sub refunded, shares returned, all assets liquidated Everyone whole; pool → Closed

See it run Everyone

The demo is reproducible in seconds on any machine with Foundry, and that reproducibility is the point. Each scenario prints what it proves, in plain units, and ends with a require-backed PASS (if the mechanism were wrong, the run would abort). The run is deterministic and self-contained: a single local Anvil chain, mock tokens standing in for USDC, the fund, the liquid sleeve and the swap venue, with epoch timing advanced through vm.warp.

$ forge script script/Demo.s.sol --sig 'run(string)' "ALL" -vvv
    S1: Happy Path - Full Lifecycle
  ==================================================
    WHAT THIS PROVES: Money flows end-to-end (launchpad -> epoch -> wind-down) with nothing lost.
    > Launchpad opens. Alice 30,000 + Bob 30,000 + Charlie 40,000 USDC deposited.
        [ok] Total locked: 100,000.00 USDC (>= 50,000 min)
    > Deadline passes -> protocol deploys capital into the 80/20 mix.
        Portfolio: 80,000.00 USDC illiquid + 20,000.00 USDC liquid | supply 100,000 shares | NAV 1.00
    > Epoch 0: Alice subscribes 10,000; Bob redeems 5,000 shares; NAV unchanged.
        [ok] Matched 5,000 P2P; net subscription 5,000 bought toward target.
    > Admin triggers wind-down; all holders redeem pro-rata.
        [ok] Final state: Closed
        [ok] Alice / Bob / Charlie USDC: 1,000,000.00 USDC / 1,000,000.00 USDC / 1,000,000.00 USDC
    RESULT: PASS - Every actor recovered their capital at NAV 1.00; supply fully retired.
  ==================================================
    S2: Launchpad Fail + Refund
  ==================================================
    WHAT THIS PROVES: If the minimum is not met, every depositor gets 100% of their USDC back.
    > Only Alice deposits 30,000 (< 50,000 minimum).
        [ok] State: LaunchpadFail (minimum not reached)
    > Alice claims her refund.
        [ok] Alice USDC restored to 1,000,000.00 USDC
    RESULT: PASS - No user funds stranded on the failure path.
  ==================================================
    S3: Cancel Pending Subscription (ERC-7887)
  ==================================================
    WHAT THIS PROVES: A user can withdraw a queued subscription before it is processed.
    > Alice subscribes 10,000, then cancels before the epoch runs.
        [ok] Pending subscription: 10,000.00 USDC
        [ok] After cancel: 0.00 USDC pending; USDC returned.
        [ok] Epoch processed as a no-op. currentEpoch = 1
    RESULT: PASS - Cancelled request refunded and skipped; queue integrity preserved.
  ==================================================
    S4: Matching - Subscription > Redemption
  ==================================================
    WHAT THIS PROVES: Subscriptions and redemptions net off P2P; only the surplus hits the fund.
        Portfolio: 80,000.00 USDC illiquid + 20,000.00 USDC liquid | supply 100,000 shares | NAV 1.00
    > Alice subscribes 10,000; Bob redeems 4,000 shares (NAV 1.00).
        [ok] Matched 4,000 P2P (no fund interaction). Net subscription 6,000.
        [ok] Rebalance buy: 4,800 illiquid + 1,200 liquid (toward 80/20).
        Portfolio: 84,800.00 USDC illiquid + 21,200.00 USDC liquid | supply 106,000 shares | NAV 1.00
        [ok] Alice received 10,000 shares; Bob received 4,000 USDC.
    RESULT: PASS - P2P netting saved a 4,000 USDC fund round-trip.
  ==================================================
    S5: Matching - Redemption > Subscription
  ==================================================
    WHAT THIS PROVES: Excess redemptions are paid from the liquid buffer, not the illiquid fund.
        Portfolio: 80,000.00 USDC illiquid + 20,000.00 USDC liquid | supply 100,000 shares | NAV 1.00
    > Alice subscribes 4,000; Bob redeems 10,000 shares (NAV 1.00).
        [ok] Matched 4,000 P2P; net redemption 6,000 paid from liquid buffer (20k -> 14k).
        Portfolio: 80,000.00 USDC illiquid + 14,000.00 USDC liquid | supply 94,000 shares | NAV 1.00
        [ok] Alice received 4,000 shares; Bob received 10,000 USDC (4k match + 6k liquid).
    RESULT: PASS - Illiquid fund never touched; buffer absorbed the redemption.
  ==================================================
    S6: Redemption Needs Illiquid Fallback
  ==================================================
    WHAT THIS PROVES: When the buffer is exhausted, redemption falls through to the illiquid fund.
    > Prior epoch drains the liquid buffer down to 2,000.
        [ok] Buffer now: 2,000.00 USDC
    > Alice redeems 8,000 shares; only 2,000 liquid is available.
        [ok] Layer 2 (liquid): 2,000 used. Layer 3 (Pruv): 6,000 redeemed.
        Portfolio: 74,000.00 USDC illiquid + 0.00 USDC liquid | supply 74,000 shares | NAV 1.00
        [ok] Alice received 8,000 USDC across both layers.
    RESULT: PASS - 3-layer redemption falls through correctly when the buffer is short.
  ==================================================
    S7: NAV Change Affects Calculations
  ==================================================
    WHAT THIS PROVES: When the underlying appreciates, redeemers are paid the higher NAV.
    > Admin marks wRWA +10% (setPrice 1.10).
        [ok] Total assets: 108,000.00 USDC | NAV: 1.08
    > Alice redeems 10 shares.
        [ok] Alice received 10.80 USDC (= 10 shares x 1.08)
    RESULT: PASS - Redemption value reflects the epoch-time NAV, not a stale price.
  ==================================================
    S8: Wind-Down Mid-Epoch
  ==================================================
    WHAT THIS PROVES: Wind-down cleanly settles all in-flight obligations; no user stranded.
    > In-flight: Alice subscription 5,000 + Bob redemption 5,000 shares (unprocessed).
    > Admin triggers wind-down.
        [ok] State: WindDown | liquidated pool: 100,000.00 USDC
        [ok] Pending subscription refunded; locked redemption shares returned.
        [ok] Final state: Closed
        [ok] Alice / Bob USDC: 1,000,000.00 USDC / 1,000,000.00 USDC
    RESULT: PASS - All obligations settled; pool closed; everyone whole at NAV 1.00.
  ##################################################
  #            ACCEPTANCE SUMMARY                  #
  ##################################################
    [PASS] S1  Happy path full lifecycle
    [PASS] S2  Launchpad fail + full refund
    [PASS] S3  Cancel pending subscription (ERC-7887)
    [PASS] S4  Matching: subscription > redemption
    [PASS] S5  Matching: redemption > subscription
    [PASS] S6  Redemption falls through to illiquid
    [PASS] S7  NAV change drives redemption value
    [PASS] S8  Wind-down settles in-flight queues
    --------------------------------------------------
    8 / 8 mechanism scenarios verified.

Under the hood For engineers

The settlement core, processEpoch(), runs matching, then the net-delta asset action, atomically. Below: the sequence, the unit convention that makes the arithmetic exact, and the test coverage behind every number above.

Decimal & NAV convention

USDC, wRWA and the liquid token are 6-decimal; vault shares are 18-decimal; price is 1e18-scaled (parity = 1e18). Mock tokens are deliberately 6-dec so mulDiv(bal, price, 1e18) stays in 6-dec USDC and setPrice(1.1e18) reads as a clean +10%.

S4 worked example: matched = min(10,000, 4,000) = 4,000; matchedShares = 4,000; net sub = 6,000; rebalance toward 80/20 on a 106,000 base → buy 4,800 illiquid + 1,200 liquid. Exact, no rounding drift.

Test coverage: 36 green

SuiteTests
Mocks (decimals, price)4
Custody (gating, swaps)7
Vault (state, queues, cancel)16
Scenarios S1–S88
Invariant (fuzz, 200 runs)1
Total36
Nothing is created or destroyed. A fuzz invariant (200 runs) checks that value is conserved across matching and settlement, that totalAssets() always covers outstanding redemptions, and that a full happy-path lifecycle ends with totalSupply == 0. Shares change only by mint (subscribe) or burn (redeem).
Engineering rigor: four internal inconsistencies in the spec were caught and resolved during implementation: the decimal scaling, S7's NAV (108k not 110k), S6's impossible setup, and S8's redeem-queue size, each documented inline in the relevant test.

Fee model For decision-makers0% in this POC, not implemented

The PRD is silent on fees, and the POC runs at 0% on purpose so the matching numbers stay clean and verifiable. This is the economic layer (deferred item Q6). It maps the decision rather than making it: every place a fee could attach, the kinds of fee and what each one is for, when each is charged, how a fee is actually taken on chain, and where comparable funds price themselves.

Every place a fee could attach

Ten candidate points sit across the lifecycle. Almost all of them converge on the single processEpoch() call, which is exactly where the design already does its work.

Launchpad

lock USDC (F1)claim shares (F2)
→

Epoch · processEpoch()

management (F10)performance (F11)exit (F4)early-exit (F5)entry (F3)cancel (F6)claim (F7)
→

Wind-down

no fee at close

Green is worth charging. Grey is deliberately held at zero, because it would either choke fundraising or punish a user for collecting their own money.

What each code means (the full catalogue, one line each)

CodeFee pointIn one lineVerdict
F1Launchpad depositcut on the USDC locked during fundraisinghold at 0
F2Claim launchpad sharesmint fewer shares than the deposithold at 0
F3Entry (subscription)bps on each subscribe at settlementhold at 0
F4Exit (redemption)bps on each redeem at settlementcharge
F5Early-exit penaltyextra % if held below N epochsfuture
F6Cancel request (7887)fee to withdraw a pending requesthold at 0
F7Claimfee when collecting already-settled fundshold at 0
F8Instant exitpremium for immediate USDC, off the epochfuture
F10Management% of NAV per year, the core feecharge
F11Performance% of gains above a high-water markdefer

F9 (a fee-on-transfer on the secondary market) was retired during review. Charge is part of the proposed model; hold at 0 is deliberately not charged; defer and future sit outside the POC. Implicit and pass-through costs (yield spread, swap spread, idle-cash, keeper, Pruv) are out of scope here.

The kinds of fee, and what each one is for

Two families. A stock fee is charged on what you hold (the assets under management). A flow fee is charged on what you move (a single subscribe or redeem). Pick the fees whose purpose fits the product and the right structure follows on its own.

FeeFamilyWhat it isWhy it exists (its job)For us
Managementstock% of NAV per year, paid by every holderPays the manager to run the fund. The backbone fee of essentially every fund, the one that keeps the lights on.core
Performancestock% of gains above a high-water markAligns the manager with investors. It earns only when holders are ahead, and the high-water mark means past losses must be recovered before it charges again.defer
Exitflowbps on the redemption valueCovers the work of serving a withdrawal. The standard, accepted fee on an async (ERC-7540) vault.optional
Early-exitflowextra % if held below N epochsProtects the holders who stay from the liquidation cost a quick in-and-out imposes. Discourages yield tourism on an asset that is genuinely illiquid.future
Entryflowbps on the subscriptionA front load. It dampens inflows, so retail products almost always set it to zero.keep 0
Reading the table. A retail-friendly evergreen-credit vault lands naturally on a management fee (the core) plus an optional exit fee, with entry held at zero, and a performance fee turned on only once the NAV is trustworthy (see the risk below). There is no menu of bundles to choose between. The job of each fee points to the answer.

When each fee is charged

Stock fees accrue a little every epoch, so the share price always reflects the fees charged to date. A flow fee is taken once, at the settlement of a redemption. Nothing is charged at claim, which only collects an amount already settled.

epoch 1
epoch 2
epoch 3
user redeem
Management 1.5%/yr
charged
charged
charged
already in the price
Performance 10%
price > peak
Exit 0.5%
charged here

How a management fee is taken: share dilution

Charging the fee means shifting a sliver of ownership to the treasury. The vault mints fee-shares, total assets do not change, and each holder's slice shrinks by exactly the fee. It costs zero liquidity, which matters when 80 to 90% of the vault sits illiquid.

feeAssets = totalAssets × mgmtBps × Δt
                       ÷ (365d × 10000)

feeShares = feeAssets × totalSupply
            ÷ (totalAssets − feeAssets)
                          ↑
            subtract feeAssets (the post-fee
            price), or the treasury is
            over-credited. The classic bug.
BeforeAfter
totalAssets$1,000,000$1,000,000
totalSupply1,000,0001,001,251.6
price / share1.000000.99875
Holders$1,000,000$998,750
Treasury$0$1,250

1.5%/yr over a one-month epoch on a $1M vault, so the fee is 1,000,000 × 1.5% × 1/12 = $1,250. Same pattern as Yearn v3, MetaMorpho and Lagoon.

Where comparable funds price themselves

Fees track the illiquidity of the underlying. Tokenization has driven treasury wrappers down to a 0.15% commodity, but private credit keeps its traditional pricing almost intact on chain. Our underlying, Hamilton Lane evergreen credit, sits at the high end.

PlatformUnderlyingMgmt / yrPerfEntryExit / early
BlackRock BUIDLT-Bills0.20–0.50%n/an/an/a
Franklin BENJIT-Bills0.15%n/an/an/a
OpenEden TBILLT-Bills0.30%n/a5 bps5 bps
Backed bIB01Bond ETF0.25%n/a20 bps20 bps
Midas mTBILLT-Bills0%10% on interest07 bps
Maple syrupUSDCPrivate creditkeeps ~15–20% of interest(included)n/aqueue free, instant ~12 bps
Securitize × HL SCOPEEvergreen private credit (closest to us)1.75% (feeder)n/a feeder0redeem at prior-quarter NAV
Hamilton Lane PAFEvergreen PE/credit1.5%10%n/an/a
TradFi interval/evergreenPrivate credit1.0–1.5%12.5% over ~5% hurdleper class2% if <12 months
Unverified. The underlying fee schedule for HL SCOPE is behind a gated prospectus. The SCOPE figures here are at the feeder layer (RWA.xyz) or inferred from the sister fund PAF. Confirm the official numbers before fixing any production rate.

Two things to get right

The real risk: a performance fee on a manual NAV. NAV is admin-submitted through setPrice. With a performance fee in place, an admin could push the price up to crystallize a phantom fee, then walk it back the next epoch, leaving holders with the loss and no refund. Precedented mitigations exist: a deviation bound on setPrice (Veda), an on-chain fee cap (Morpho and Lagoon cap at 50%), rates that can only fall after deploy (Lagoon), and a timelock on parameter changes. The root cause is the manual NAV, which is why this ties directly to the INavSource seam and the oracle decision.
An implementation detail: charge flow fees on the gross of each request. Entry and exit fees must be taken on each request before matching. Charging on the post-match net would let matched volume slip through untaxed. Stock fees (management and performance) are immune, because their base is the assets under management, which matching does not change.

Scope, simplifications & what's next For decision-makers

In scope, and proven

  • 5-state lifecycle & one-way transitions
  • Async subscription/redemption queues
  • Cancel pending request (ERC-7887)
  • P2P matching, both directions
  • 3-layer redemption fallback
  • Rebalance-toward-target asset mix
  • Full wind-down settlement

Deliberately out of scope (POC)

  • UI, mainnet/testnet deployment
  • Cross-chain bridge, real Pruv API
  • NAV oracle (price is admin-submitted)
  • Role-based access, fee model (0%)
  • Curve formula (AMM is 1:1), Aave reinvest
  • Audit hardening, gas optimisation
Known POC simplifications: the admin must submit the price before each epoch (not yet enforced in code); the fund mock always fills redemptions fully; the AMM swaps 1:1. Each is a deliberate L1 choice and a clear first task for the next phase.

Suggested next phases

  • L2 integration shape: fork mainnet, real Aave/Curve mocks, multi-chain wiring.
  • Real Pruv integration + a NAV oracle (replacing manual setPrice), with a staleness guard.
  • Hardening: security audit, gas pass, fee model, role-based access.
Retail Access Vault, a local proof-of-concept (Alternative 1). Not a product: no mainnet, no audit, no real fund integration. Source spec: docs/05-spec.md. Generated from the live test suite and demo run.
